planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; scarlet honey myrtle is native to the seasonally moist, sandy soils of southwestern Western Australia and is more tolerant of summer moisture than many other Australian natives. It blooms in spring and early summer, producing vivid, brilliant-red, bottlebrush-like flower spikes outstanding for nectar-feeding birds. Several natural flower-color variants exist: scarlet-red, salmon-orange, pink, and purple-lilac.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet honey myrtle is a medium-sized Australian shrub that produces in spring and early summer one of the most vivid and brilliant-red bottlebrush-type flower displays in the Melaleuca genus: dense, cylindrical, bottlebrush-like spikes of bright, true-scarlet to vivid red flowers composed of numerous stamen bundles projecting from the central axis, each cluster of stamens bright scarlet-red, the whole spike a dense, vivid, bristling cylinder of saturated red from 2–3 inches long. The flower color is among the most intense, pure reds of any commonly cultivated Australian shrub. Natural color variants also occur within the species — including salmon, pink, and purple-lilac forms — providing additional color options from the same species. Like other Melaleuca species it produces nectar that is highly attractive to honeyeating birds in Australia and hummingbirds in cultivation, the bottlebrush structure perfectly adapted for bird pollination by providing a stable perch while the bird probes for nectar. Somewhat more moisture-tolerant than many Australian natives, making it more adaptable to the wet summer conditions of the Gulf Coast in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; mountain devil requires excellent drainage and no phosphorus fertilizer (like all Proteaceae). It is a slow-growing shrub with unusual, distinctively shaped seed pods — woody follicles with prominent curved, curved horns at the opening — that have given it the common name 'mountain devil' for the devil-like horned silhouette of the pods. The scarlet, tubular flowers bloom intermittently throughout the year in mild climates.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; mountain devil is an Eastern Australian Proteaceae shrub of the Sydney sandstone heathlands that has become well-known for a botanical curiosity: the woody seed pods, when mature and dried, have a distinctive shape with two prominent, curved, tusk-like horns projecting from the opening of each follicle — the overall silhouette of the pod resembling a tiny devil or horned face, giving the plant its memorable and accurate common name. These distinctive horned seed pods have made mountain devil a popular dried-floral material in Australia; the pods are collected, sometimes dyed, and used in wreaths and arrangements. In the garden it is also a rewarding ornamental plant producing throughout the year (most heavily in late winter and spring) terminal clusters of 7 tubular, scarlet-red flowers with prominent, recurving, scarlet petals — vivid, hummingbird-attracting flowers of considerable color impact. Like all Proteaceae it requires excellent drainage and no phosphorus fertilizer. The combination of attractive red flowers and the remarkable, collectible, horned seed pods makes it a plant of multiple ornamental seasons.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds or transplant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, sandy soil in full sun; mulla mulla is native to the arid interior of Australia and requires excellent drainage and lean, dry conditions. It is often grown as an annual or short-lived perennial. In humid-summer climates it may decline after flowering — treat as an annual. The fluffy, pink, bottlebrush-like flower spikes have made it popular as a cut flower export from Australia.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy soil in full sun; mulla mulla is an Australian arid-zone plant of striking visual character — native to the dry inland regions of Western Australia, South Australia, and the Northern Territory, where it blooms after rain in remarkable mass-flowering events that color the usually bare, red-soiled landscape with waves of fluffy pink. The flower spikes are cylindrical to bottle-brush-shaped, 3–6 inches long, composed of hundreds of densely packed, feathery-textured, silvery-pink to rose-pink individual flowers with prominent, silky, feathery bristles that give the whole spike an extraordinarily soft, furry, fluffy texture — exactly like a large pink pussy willow or a fluffy cylindrical brush. Each spike shimmers in the slightest breeze as the silky feathery bracts catch the light. The common name 'mulla mulla' is from the local Aboriginal language of the region. In cut flower trade, the distinctive fluffy pink spikes export well as dried or fresh flowers for the floral industry, where they provide texture and soft pink color unavailable from any other commonly grown cut flower. In the garden it provides a dry-area, full-sun, pink-spike display of entirely unusual texture and form.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; common net bush is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the hot, dry, lean conditions of southwestern Australian coastal heath. It blooms mainly in spring to early summer, producing distinctive, one-sided clusters of crimson flower bundles embedded along the woody stems. More cold-tolerant than many Western Australian natives. The common name 'net bush' refers to the appearance of older specimens.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; common net bush is a large Australian shrub of the southwest with a distinctly unusual flower arrangement — unlike the symmetrical bottlebrush spikes of Callistemon, in which flowers radiate from all sides of a terminal spike, Calothamnus (and its flowers are embedded along the woody, older stems and oriented predominantly to one side of the stem — the 'one-sided bottlebrush' habit that makes the genus immediately distinguishable. The crimson flower bundles appear in spring along the older, needle-leaved, dark-green stems at intervals, the scarlet to crimson stamen bundles projecting to one side of the stem in a bold, contrasting display of rich crimson on dark, needle-like foliage. The effect is unusual and distinctly architectural — red flower bundles appearing at intervals along the dark, spiky-leaved, one-sided bottlebrush stems — and quite different from the familiar symmetrical bottlebrush form. It is extremely drought-tolerant once established, enduring the hot, dry, lean conditions of southwestern Australian coastal scrub without supplemental irrigation in Mediterranean-climate zones. A distinctive choice for water-wise zone 9 gardens seeking Australian flora with unusual flower arrangement.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; gravel bottlebrush is native to the seasonally wet, sandy, gravelly soils of southwestern Western Australia — tolerating periodic waterlogging better than most Australian shrubs, which makes it more adaptable to the wet-summer Gulf Coast conditions of zone 9 than many Australian natives. It blooms in summer (November–February in Australia), providing summer bottlebrush interest. Outstanding for birds.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; gravel bottlebrush is a Western Australian shrub of the Myrtaceae family — related to the true bottlebrushes (Callistemon) and honeymrtles (Melaleuca) — that produces in summer vivid, spherical to rounded, bottlebrush-type flower heads of brilliant orange to orange-red composed of densely massed, projecting stamen bundles in warm, vivid orange, each flower head 2–3 inches across and the plant in full bloom covered in multiple such heads of intense orange. Unlike many Australian shrubs that are summer-dry and drought-tolerant, gravel bottlebrush is native to the seasonally wet, gravelly, sandy flats that experience periodic winter-spring flooding — and therefore tolerates summer moisture and periodic wet conditions significantly better than most Australian ornamentals. This moisture tolerance makes it more suitable for the humid-summer conditions of the zone 9 Gulf Coast than the strictly dry-climate Australian shrubs. The summer bloom timing provides vivid orange bottlebrush flowers in the summer garden season — a different and valuable window from the spring-blooming Australian genera. Outstanding for birds and bees.
